rationale: The citing paper gives Wang and Lee (2006) a detailed treatment in its boundary-conditions discussion, summarizing the study setup and key results rather than listing it as background. It uses that paper as direct empirical evidence that regulatory-fit effects in preference construction are stronger under low involvement and tied to selective attention and perceived feature attractiveness. The authors then leverage this evidence to frame an unresolved theoretical issue (heuristic vs. systematic processing and motivation), showing a meaningful conceptual role in the argument.
relation_description: Aaker and Lee use Wang and Lee (2006) as empirical grounding for a central claim that involvement moderates regulatory-fit effects, with fit-based selectivity emerging mainly under low motivation. This evidence anchors their conceptual discussion of boundary conditions and motivates further theory development on processing mechanisms.
